step_by_step_thinking:
\boxed{B}
Mitochondria do not transfer from cell to cell in the manner described in the query. The evidence supports mitochondrial functionality—such as the role of cytochrome c in apoptosis and electron transport—and describes intra-cellular mechanisms involving protein targeting, membrane permeabilization, and organelle-specific enzymatic activities. There is no mention or support for intercellular transfer of entire mitochondria, making option B correct. Mitochondrial components like cytochrome c are released during events such as MOMP (outer mitochondrial membrane permeabilization), but they are not passed intact or whole to adjacent cells. Instead, signaling cascades occur locally within the dying cell. Therefore, mitochondria themselves do not transfer between cells under normal physiological conditions.
raw_response:
\boxed{B}
Mitochondria do not transfer from cell to cell in the manner described in the query. The evidence supports mitochondrial functionality—such as the role of cytochrome c in apoptosis and electron transport—and describes intra-cellular mechanisms involving protein targeting, membrane permeabilization, and organelle-specific enzymatic activities. There is no mention or support for intercellular transfer of entire mitochondria, making option B correct. Mitochondrial components like cytochrome c are released during events such as MOMP (outer mitochondrial membrane permeabilization), but they are not passed intact or whole to adjacent cells. Instead, signaling cascades occur locally within the dying cell. Therefore, mitochondria themselves do not transfer between cells under normal physiological conditions. \boxed{B}
